1: The list of remaining nations serves the dual purpose stated in vv2-4: teaching war to the new generation and testing Israel's covenant loyalty. The five Philistine lords (sərānê pəlištîm — the word may be related to Greek tyrannos, tyrant) correspond to the five city-states of the Philistine Pentapolis: Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron. The Philistines were Sea Peoples who arrived on the Levantine coast around 1200 BCE as part of the broader Late Bronze Age collapse — the same population movement that destroyed Ugarit, ended Hittite power, and attempted to invade Egypt (documented on the reliefs of Ramesses III at Medinet Habu). Their appearance in Judges thus coincides historically with their settlement of the coastal plain, making the Judges narratives a window into the first centuries of Philistine-Israelite interaction.
7: Othniel son of Kenaz is presented as the paradigmatic judge: called, empowered by the Spirit, victorious, the land rests forty years. The structure of his story (vv7-11) is the Deuteronomic cycle in its purest, most compact form — eight verses containing all the elements. Cushan-rishathaim king of Aram-naharaim ('Cushan of Double Wickedness from the Two Rivers') — the name is almost certainly a scribal pejorative, substituting rishʿatayim (double wickedness) for his actual name. Aram-naharaim (Mesopotamia between the Tigris and Euphrates) fits the Hittite/Mitanni vacuum of the early 12th century BCE. Othniel's connection to the Caleb clan (Judg 1:13) establishes his Judahite credentials, making him the southern exemplar of the judge tradition.
12: Eglon king of Moab forms an unlikely coalition with Ammon and Amalek to strike Israel. Moab occupies the Transjordan plateau east of the Dead Sea; the Moabite Stone (Mesha Stele, 9th century BCE) documents Moabite-Israelite conflict from the Moabite perspective, providing background for understanding the Moabite relationship to Israel across this period. Eglon means 'calf/bull' — the fat-bull imagery of v17 ('now Eglon was a very fat man') may be deliberate satire on a bovine king. His 18-year oppression is the longest named oppression in the pre-Gideon narratives. The coalition taking the 'City of Palms' (Jericho region) extends Moabite control west of the Jordan into Benjamin's territory — a reversal of the Transjordan conquests under Moses.
15: Ehud son of Gera, a Benjaminite, is described as left-handed ('restricted in his right hand'). The connection between Benjaminite warriors and left-handedness appears again in Judg 20:16 (700 left-handed slingers). Being left-handed was advantageous for concealing weapons on the right hip (where a left-handed sword draw from the left hip would not be checked in the standard body search). The assassination scene is constructed with narrative relish: the private summer room, the guards who assume Eglon is 'covering his feet' (a euphemism for defecating), the delay giving Ehud time to escape — the narrator crafts a political satire celebrating Ehud's cunning against the fat foreign oppressor.
20: 'I have a message from God for you' — Ehud's declaration to Eglon inverts the scene: the 'tribute bearer' becomes the divine messenger; the royal throne room becomes the site of execution. Eglon 'rose from his seat' in response to the divine-message formula — an act of reverence toward YHWH's word that was also the act that exposed him to the blade. The detail that 'the fat closed over the blade' (v22) and that he 'died in the latrine' (v22-23) is deliberately undignified — ANE royal ideology presented kings as semi-divine, larger-than-life; the Ehud narrative systematically mocks Eglon, presenting him as fat, credulous, and dying in his own excrement. This is resistance literature: Israel's humor about their oppressor.
31: Shamgar son of Anath kills 600 Philistines with an oxgoad. He is mentioned again in the Song of Deborah (5:6) as a parallel to Jael. His non-Israelite name (Shamgar is a Hurrian name; Anath is a Canaanite goddess) has led some scholars to identify him as a Canaanite or mercenary rather than an Israelite — if so, he is a non-covenant figure whose actions YHWH uses for Israel's deliverance, a pattern that foreshadows Rahab (Josh 2), Jael (Judg 4-5), and Ruth. The oxgoad — a long wooden pole with a metal point used to drive oxen — is the agricultural implement of a farmer pressed into impromptu service. The 600 Philistines killed anticipates the scale of Samson's era conflicts. Shamgar is presented without the Spirit-empowerment formula or the full Deuteronomic cycle, suggesting historical memory preserved in the tradition rather than a theologized judge narrative.
2: The 'teaching war' rationale for leaving nations in the land — 'so that the generations of the people of Israel might know war, to teach war to those who had not known it before' — is a second, complementary justification alongside the testing rationale of v4. The concern is practical: a generation born in the land, without experience of the conquest campaigns, needed military formation. This dual rationale (testing covenant fidelity + military training) reflects the Deuteronomistic framing of Israel's history as a period of both moral and martial preparation for the monarchy.
3: The list of remaining nations — the five Philistine lords, all Canaanites, Sidonians, and Hivites on Mount Lebanon to Lebo-hamath — represents a geographic sweep from the coastal plain (Philistines) to the Phoenician coast (Sidonians) to the northern Lebanese Beqa' valley (Hivites/Hamath). The 'five lords of the Philistines' (sĕranê pĕlištîm) is a specific political title — the Philistine pentapolis was governed by city-lords rather than kings, a political structure distinctive to Philistine governance, possibly related to their Aegean origins.
4: The testing rationale is made explicit: 'to test Israel by them, whether Israel would obey the commandments of YHWH, which he commanded their fathers by Moses.' The phrase 'by Moses' (bĕyad mōšê, literally 'by the hand of Moses') anchors the test in the specific covenant mediation of Sinai — it is not an abstract moral examination but a test against the specific commands delivered through a known covenant mediator. This frames the Judges period as a Mosaic-covenant examination period whose results will determine the shape of Israel's subsequent history.
5: Israel's dwelling 'among the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites' — the six-nation list — recapitulates the conquest target list of Deut 7:1 and Josh 3:10. The list functions as a literary marker: by naming all six nations as Israel's neighbors rather than defeated enemies, the text signals complete covenantal failure in the settlement project. The people YHWH had commanded to destroy or displace are now the people among whom Israel lives on equal terms.
6: Intermarriage with the Canaanites and their gods is the ultimate stage of assimilation that Deut 7:3-4 had specifically forbidden: 'you shall not intermarry with them... for they would turn away your sons from following me.' The verse confirms that Israel had crossed every boundary — territorial separation, cultic separation, and now familial separation — creating the conditions for the complete religious syncretism that the prophets will later condemn. The worship of Canaanite gods that follows in vv7ff. is the inevitable downstream effect of the intermarriage the law had prohibited.
8: Cushan-rishathaim of Aram-naharaim is the first of the oppressing foreign powers. His name meaning 'doubly wicked Cushite' (rišātayim = double wickedness) is almost certainly a polemical epithet substituted for his real name by the Israelite tradition. Aram-naharaim ('Aram of the two rivers') is the upper Mesopotamian region known as Paddan-aram in the patriarchal narratives — the homeland of Rebekah and Leah and Rachel. The eight-year servitude (šmōneh šānîm) is the first of the stylized numbers that structure the Judges cycles.
9: Othniel is 'raised up' (wayyāqem) by YHWH as a deliverer — the same Hiphil of qum used for YHWH's acts of raising prophets and kings throughout the Deuteronomistic History. His identity as 'Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother' connects his emergence to the conquest hero already introduced in ch1 — the judges are not random charismatic figures but emerge from the families and clan-networks already established as faithful in the settlement narratives. The cry of Israel (zāʿaq) is the covenant signal: suffering people appealing to their covenant God.
10: The Spirit of YHWH (rûaḥ Yhwh) coming upon Othniel is the charismatic anointing that defines the judges as a category distinct from kings or priests: their authority is not hereditary, institutional, or geographical but immediate and pneumatic. The same Spirit pattern governs Gideon (6:34), Jephthah (11:29), and Samson (13:25; 14:6, 19; 15:14). The Spirit empowers him to 'judge Israel' and then 'go out to war' — the judicial and military functions are combined in the judge's role, distinguishing Israelite governance in this period from both the elder-council model and the later monarchic model.
11: The forty-year rest period is a stylized round number representing one full generation. 'The land had rest forty years' (watiššqōṭ hāʾāreṣ ʾarbaʿîm šānâ) — the subject is the land rather than the people, reflecting a concept of the land itself experiencing peace or disturbance in resonance with its inhabitants' covenant status (cf. Lev 26:34-35, where the land 'enjoys its sabbaths' during exile). The rest period ends with Othniel's death, not with renewed apostasy directly — the cycle is reset when the charismatic leader dies and his institutionalizing effect on the community dissolves.
13: Eglon's coalition with Ammon and Amalek creates a Transjordanian alliance that takes 'the city of palms' (Jericho, cf. Deut 34:3). Jericho's capture by Moab is particularly resonant: the first city taken by Israel in the conquest is now retaken by a foreign power, reversing the conquest and symbolically returning Israel to pre-conquest vulnerability. Ammon and Amalek were traditional enemies of Israel in the Transjordan and Sinai respectively; their combination with Moab represents a comprehensive eastern and southern threat.
14: Eighteen years of servitude to Eglon is one of the shorter oppression periods in Judges (cf. eight years under Cushan, forty years under Philistines in 13:1). The varying lengths of the oppression periods — 8, 18, 20, 7, 40, 18, 40 years — are stylized but not uniform, suggesting the tradition preserved memory of different degrees of severity. Moab's oppression is centered on the Benjamin-Jericho area (the 'city of palms') rather than extending across all Israel, which explains why Ehud, a Benjaminite, is the deliverer.
16: Ehud's dagger was 'a cubit long' (gōmed — a unit of about 12 inches, possibly a short forearm-length) and double-edged. Its concealment on his right thigh — where a left-handed man's sword hand would not naturally reach — was the tactical advantage that made the assassination possible: Eglon's guards would check the left hip for a right-hander's sword and find nothing. The detail reflects an ancient awareness of the security advantage of left-handedness as a form of asymmetry that exploits standard security assumptions.
17: The narrator's note that Eglon was 'a very fat man' (ʾîš bārîʾ mĕʾōd) is one of the Bible's most vivid physical descriptions of an antagonist. The fatness serves several narrative functions: it explains why the dagger disappears completely in v22 (the fat closes over the blade), it may symbolize the prosperity of an oppressor living off Israel's tribute, and it creates dark comedy appropriate to this trickster narrative. Ancient Hebrew narrative rarely comments on physical appearance without narrative purpose; here the detail is load-bearing for the plot.
18: When Ehud had finished presenting the tribute, he sent away the people who had carried it — leaving himself alone with Eglon. The staging is deliberate: Ehud creates the private access he needs for assassination by using the tribute ceremony's natural endpoint as an opportunity to disperse witnesses. The public tribute presentation gave him legitimate access to the king's presence; the dismissal of the bearers removed the witnesses.
19: Ehud 'turned back from the idols at Gilgal' — he had apparently departed with the bearers toward Gilgal but returned, claiming to have a secret message. The 'carved images' (pĕsîlîm) near Gilgal may have been standing stones associated with the Gilgal sanctuary (cf. Josh 4:20). His invocation of a divine message — 'I have a word of God for you' (dĕbar ʾĕlōhîm lî ʾēlêkā) — causes Eglon to command silence, clear the room, and stand up in anticipation of divine speech. The irony: the 'word of God' will be delivered through iron rather than voice.
21: Ehud 'reached with his left hand and took the sword from his right thigh and thrust it into his belly.' The narrative reversal is complete: the tribute-bearer becomes the executioner; the sword concealed in the wrong place (right thigh for a left-hander) is drawn at the critical moment; the king who commands Israelite tribute receives Israelite steel. The scene is deliberately ironic throughout — the assassin acts as if he is a messenger from the divine king while serving the human king who oppresses YHWH's people.
22: 'The handle also went in after the blade, and the fat closed over the blade, for he did not pull the sword out of his belly; and the dung came out.' This is the Bible's most graphically detailed assassination scene, with a dark scatological humor characteristic of some Israelite trickster narratives (cf. the Elijah-Baal contest mockery in 1 Kings 18:27). The fat closing over the blade explains the disappeared weapon; the 'dung' signals that the belly-thrust had perforated the intestines. The graphic detail serves to deflate Eglon's royal dignity and affirm his death beyond doubt.
23: Ehud escapes through the 'porch' (misdĕrônâ) — a hapax legomenon of uncertain meaning, possibly a vestibule, portico, or private passage in the upper chamber architecture. The exact escape route is unclear, which has generated extensive ancient and modern discussion. What is clear is that Ehud exited the locked inner room before the servants discovered Eglon's body — the architectural detail is preserved because it explained how Ehud vanished despite the locked doors that confused the servants.
24: The servants' assumption that Eglon was 'relieving himself' (mēsîk raglāyw, literally 'covering his feet') — a euphemism for defecation in a private chamber — is the pivot of the black comedy. The locked doors signaled privacy; privacy in an inner chamber after a meal was reasonably interpreted as bathroom use. Their restraint in waiting — 'they waited until they were embarrassed' — gives Ehud precisely the time he needs to escape. The servants' embarrassment (bōšû) at interrupting their king has fatal political consequences.
25: When the servants finally take a key (hammapteaḥ — perhaps the only occurrence of 'key' in this sense in Biblical Hebrew) and open the doors, they find their king 'fallen dead on the floor' — the word nāp̄al (fallen) is used throughout Judges for those slain in battle, giving the discovery scene a battlefield vocabulary. The discovery ends the black comedy and pivots to Ehud's military campaign — the assassination was the precondition for the military deliverance, not an end in itself.
26: Ehud escaped to Seirah while the servants delayed — his escape was directly enabled by the confusion and delay in the palace. Seirah's location is unknown but appears to be in the Ephraim hill country based on the narrative sequence (v27 mentions Ephraim). The geography of Ehud's operation spans the Jericho plain (Eglon's palace), the Gilgal region (the idol-stones), and the Ephraim highlands (Seirah) — Ehud moved through Moabite-controlled territory using the legitimate cover of a tribute mission.
27: The trumpet blast (yiqqāʿ bašōpār) in the Ephraim hill country is the standard military signal for mustering troops (cf. Num 10:9; Josh 6:4-5; Judg 6:34). Ehud's trumpet transforms his personal act of assassination into a national moment of military opportunity — the Moabites at the fords are now leaderless and their retreat route is about to be cut. The combination of assassination + swift troop mobilization + seizure of crossing points is a sophisticated operational plan, not merely opportunistic fighting.
28: 'Seize the fords of the Jordan against the Moabites and let no one pass' — the tactical key. The Jordan fords were the only practical crossing points; controlling them trapped the Moabite force west of the Jordan without a retreat route. The same tactical logic appears in Gideon's operation (7:24-25) and throughout the conquest and Judges period. The command 'the LORD has given your enemies the Moabites into your hand' is the standard holy-war declaration that signals YHWH's involvement in the battle and authorizes Israelite aggression.
29: The killing of approximately 10,000 Moabites — described as 'all strong, able-bodied men; not a man escaped' — is presented as a comprehensive military victory. The emphasis on the completeness of the slaughter ('not a man escaped') echoes the ḥērem language of total-war ideology. The Moabite force trapped between Ehud's pursuers and the sealed Jordan fords was annihilated rather than driven back — a military operation that removed Moab's military capacity rather than merely their occupation force.
30: 'So Moab was subdued that day under the hand of Israel, and the land had rest for eighty years' — the eighty years (double the normal forty) is the longest peace period in Judges, suggesting either that Ehud's deliverance was particularly thorough or that the tradition preserved a memory of an unusually long period of stability. The completion formula 'the land had rest' (watiššqōṭ hāʾāreṣ) closes the Ehud cycle with the same phrase as the Othniel cycle (v11), confirming that the second judge's deliverance met the same covenantal standard as the paradigmatic first judge's.
